summary: Trigger-phrase sanitization accepts compatibility-form and hidden-character instruction strings that Gate 3 and shared-provenance collapse to dangerous ASCII and reject.
Suspicious-prefix extraction filters can be bypassed with compatibility digits because trigger-phrase sanitization stays on NFC while later surfaces collapse to ASCII digits.
Shared-provenance strip patterns miss Greek-omicron instruction text; `ignοre previous` survives both trigger-phrase sanitization and recovered-payload sanitization and reaches hook output.

relation: surviving recovered-payload text bypasses strip patterns and encounters no downstream content-aware Zod validation before hook output.

summary: No reverse mismatch was found in the tested corpus where trigger-phrase sanitization rejected a Unicode payload but shared-provenance preserved it for instruction delivery; the observed asymmetry is consistently trigger-weaker or shared-provenance-broader.
